questions.text: Who drafted the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar chaired the Drafting Committee of the Indian Constitution.

questions.text: The Magna Carta was signed in 1215 and limited the powers of the English King.
questions.explanation: The Magna Carta was indeed signed in 1215 by King John.

questions.text: Which article of the Indian Constitution grants Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Articles 12–35 of the Indian Constitution outline the Fundamental Rights granted to all citizens.

questions.text: In which year did India achieve independence from British rule?
questions.explanation: India gained independence on August 15, 1947.

questions.text: The French Revolution occurred in the 18th century.
questions.explanation: The French Revolution took place from 1789 to 1799.

questions.text: The Indian Constitution was adopted on ______ 26, 1950.
questions.explanation: The Indian Constitution came into effect on January 26, 1950, which is celebrated as Republic Day.

questions.text: Who was the first President of independent India?
questions.explanation: Rajendra Prasad was the first President of India from 1950 to 1962.

questions.text: Which of the following are Fundamental Rights granted by the Indian Constitution? (Select all correct)
options: B. Right to Freedom of Religion
questions.explanation: Rights to Equality, Freedom of Religion, and Constitutional Remedies are Fundamental Rights. Exploitation and unlimited wealth are not rights granted.

questions.text: Assertion: The French Revolution influenced democratic movements worldwide. Reason: It established ideals of liberty, equality, and fraternity.
questions.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are correct. The French Revolution's principles inspired global democratization.

questions.text: Who was known as the 'Father of the Indian Nation'?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi is revered as the Father of the Indian Nation for his role in independence.

questions.text: The American Revolution occurred before the French Revolution.
questions.explanation: The American Revolution (1775–1783) preceded the French Revolution (1789–1799).

questions.text: The doctrine of Satyagraha, or non-violent resistance, was championed by ______.
questions.explanation: Satyagraha, meaning truth and non-violence, was Gandhi's powerful tool against British colonialism.

questions.text: Which ancient civilization built the Great Wall?
questions.explanation: China built the Great Wall over many centuries to protect against invasions.

questions.text: Match historical figures with their major contributions:
pairs: End of apartheid in South Africa
questions.explanation: Each historical figure made significant contributions to their respective nations and the world.

questions.text: In which year was the Preamble to the Indian Constitution adopted?
questions.explanation: The Preamble to the Indian Constitution was adopted on November 26, 1949.

questions.text: The British East India Company was primarily a military organization.
questions.explanation: The British East India Company was a trading company that eventually became a colonial power.

questions.text: The fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989 symbolized the end of the ______ War.
questions.explanation: The fall of the Berlin Wall marked the symbolic end of the Cold War between the Soviet Union and Western powers.

questions.text: Which empire built the Colosseum in Rome?
questions.explanation: The Colosseum was built during the Roman Empire, completed in 80 AD.

questions.text: Assertion: The American Declaration of Independence was signed in 1776. Reason: It proclaimed the thirteen colonies' independence from British rule.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. The Declaration of Independence was signed on July 4, 1776.

questions.text: Which of the following were key events in the Indian Independence Movement? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: The Salt March, Quit India Movement, and Rebellion of 1857 were key independence events. Plassey was a British victory, and Versailles was post-WWI.

questions.text: Who wrote the American Constitution?
options: C. James Madison and the Founding Fathers
questions.explanation: The American Constitution was drafted by James Madison and other Founding Fathers at the Constitutional Convention of 1787.

questions.text: The Renaissance period in Europe occurred during the Middle Ages.
questions.explanation: The Renaissance (14th–17th centuries) followed the Middle Ages and marked the transition to modern Europe.

questions.text: The Treaty of ______ ended World War I in 1918.
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles was signed on June 28, 1919, formally ending WWI.

questions.text: Which ancient wonder of the world still stands today?
questions.explanation: The Great Pyramid of Giza is the only ancient wonder still largely intact.

questions.text: Assertion: The Industrial Revolution transformed economies from agrarian to industrial. Reason: It introduced mechanization and factory systems.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. The Industrial Revolution fundamentally changed economic and social structures.

questions.text: Match empires with their characteristics:
pairs: Emperor Ashoka and early Indian unity
questions.explanation: These empires left distinct cultural, architectural, and political legacies in India.

questions.text: In which year did the Jallianwala Bagh massacre occur?
questions.explanation: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre occurred on April 13, 1919, in Amritsar.

questions.text: The Indus Valley Civilization is one of the world's oldest known civilizations.
questions.explanation: The Indus Valley Civilization (2600–1900 BCE) is one of the world's oldest urban civilizations.

questions.text: The ______ Dynasty ruled the Mughal Empire and left a lasting impact on Indian architecture.
questions.explanation: The Mughal Dynasty ruled from 1526 to 1857 and built iconic structures like the Taj Mahal.

questions.text: Who was the first Emperor of the Maurya Empire?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya founded the Maurya Empire around 322 BCE.

questions.text: Which of the following were significant women in Indian history? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: Rani Lakshmibai, Savitribai Phule, and Indira Gandhi were significant Indian historical figures. Queen Victoria was British.

questions.text: Assertion: The Vedic period is the foundation of Hindu civilization. Reason: The Vedas were the first sacred texts written in Sanskrit.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. The Vedas form the basis of Hindu philosophy and Indian culture.

questions.text: Which movement advocated for Swadeshi (self-reliance) during the Indian Independence struggle?
questions.explanation: The Swadeshi Movement encouraged Indians to use indigenous products and boycott British goods.

questions.text: The Roman Empire fell completely in 476 AD.
questions.explanation: The Western Roman Empire fell in 476 AD, marking the end of the classical period.

questions.text: The ______ Edict of Ashoka promoted Buddhist teachings across his empire.
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Pillars contained edicts that spread Buddhist teachings and moral teachings throughout the Maurya Empire.

questions.text: In which battle did the British defeat Tipu Sultan?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Seringapatam (1799) resulted in Tipu Sultan's defeat and death.

questions.text: Assertion: The partition of India in 1947 created the nations of India and Pakistan. Reason: Religious and communal tensions necessitated the division.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. Partition was driven by communal differences and led to massive population displacement.

questions.text: Match important Indian historical dates with events:
pairs: Formation of the Indian National Congress
questions.explanation: These dates mark pivotal moments in India's journey toward independence and nation-building.

questions.text: Who was the first Prime Minister of India?
questions.explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru served as the first Prime Minister of India from 1947 to 1964.

questions.text: The printing press was invented by Johannes Gutenberg in the 15th century.
questions.explanation: Gutenberg invented the movable type printing press around 1440, revolutionizing information dissemination.

questions.text: The ______ Pact of 1942 was a failed attempt to resolve political deadlock during India's independence struggle.
questions.explanation: The Cripps Mission of 1942 proposed dominion status but was rejected by Indian leaders seeking complete independence.

questions.text: Which treaty ended the Napoleonic Wars?
questions.explanation: The Congress of Vienna (1815) and resulting treaties ended the Napoleonic Wars.

questions.text: Assertion: The Enlightenment period promoted reason and scientific thinking. Reason: Philosophers like Voltaire and Rousseau challenged traditional authority.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. Enlightenment thinkers advocated rationalism and individual liberty.

questions.text: Which of the following were features of the Mughal administrative system? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: Mansabdari system, Persian language, and centralized bureaucracy were key features. Federal and democratic elements were absent.

questions.text: Who led the Revolt of 1857 from Kanpur?
questions.explanation: Nana Sahib led the rebellion from Kanpur and was one of the revolt's major leaders.

questions.text: The Western Roman Empire and Eastern Byzantine Empire coexisted for centuries.
questions.explanation: After 395 AD, the Roman Empire was divided, with the Byzantine Empire lasting until 1453.

questions.text: The ______ was the most significant anti-colonial movement launched by Gandhi in 1942.
questions.explanation: The Quit India Movement demanded immediate British withdrawal from India.

questions.text: Which ruler unified most of India under the Maurya Empire?
questions.explanation: Emperor Ashoka expanded the Maurya Empire to its greatest extent, ruling most of the Indian subcontinent.

questions.text: Assertion: The Magna Carta established the principle that the king is subject to law. Reason: It limited royal power through written agreements.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. The Magna Carta was foundational to constitutional governance.

questions.text: Match historical figures with their respective nations:
questions.explanation: These leaders were instrumental in shaping their nations during periods of major political change.

questions.text: Who authored the book 'The Discovery of India'?
questions.explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru wrote 'The Discovery of India' while imprisoned during the independence struggle.

questions.text: The American Civil War was fought between 1861 and 1865.
questions.explanation: The American Civil War occurred from 1861 to 1865, resulting in the abolition of slavery.

questions.text: Which of the following were significant revolts against British rule in India? (Select all correct)
questions.explanation: Sepoy Mutiny, Moplah Rebellion, Khilafat Movement, and Civil Disobedience Movement were significant revolts. Treaty of Amritsar was a peace treaty.

explanation: Articles 12–35 of the Indian Constitution deal with Fundamental Rights.

explanation.text: Match the independence leaders with their independence movements:
explanation.explanation: These leaders are associated with their respective freedom movements.

explanation.text: The Sepoy Mutiny occurred in which year?
explanation.explanation: The Revolt of 1857 began in May 1857.

explanation.text: The French Revolution took place in the 18th century.
explanation.explanation: The French Revolution began in 1789.

explanation.text: Which of these were Mughal emperors? (Select all correct)
explanation.explanation: Akbar, Shah Jahan, and Aurangzeb were Mughal emperors.

explanation.text: Assertion: Democracy requires a constitution. Reason: A constitution defines the structure and powers of government.
explanation.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

explanation.text: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted in which year?
explanation.explanation: The UDHR was adopted by the UN General Assembly on 10 December 1948.

explanation.text: The Indian Constitution currently has ______ Schedules.
explanation.explanation: The Constitution of India currently contains 12 Schedules.

explanation.text: In which year did the First World War begin?
explanation.explanation: The First World War began in 1914 following the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand.

explanation.text: The Industrial Revolution first began in Great Britain.
explanation.explanation: The Industrial Revolution originated in Great Britain in the late 18th century before spreading globally.

explanation.text: The Quit India Movement was launched in the year ________.
explanation.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi launched the Quit India Movement on August 8, 1942, demanding an end to British rule.

explanation.text: Which of the following nations were part of the Axis Powers during World War II? (Select all correct)
explanation.explanation: Germany, Italy, and Japan formed the core of the Axis Powers during World War II.

explanation.text: Assertion: The Cold War did not involve direct large-scale military combat between the USA and the USSR. Reason: Both superpowers possessed nuclear weapons, leading to the doctrine of Mutually Assured Destruction.
explanation.explanation: Both the assertion and the reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why direct military conflict was avoided.

explanation.text: Match the prominent Indian historical figures with their popular titles:
explanation.explanation: These leaders were affectionately given these titles by the public and their contemporaries for their roles in India's independence.

explanation.text: Who was the first President of the United States of America?
explanation.explanation: George Washington served as the first President of the United States from 1789 to 1797.

explanation.text: The United Nations was formed immediately after World War I in 1919.
explanation.explanation: The League of Nations was formed in 1919. The United Nations was formed in 1945 after World War II.

explanation.text: Article 14 of the Indian Constitution guarantees the Right to ________.
explanation.explanation: Article 14 states that the State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws.

explanation.text: The Russian Revolution, which led to the rise of the Soviet Union, occurred in which year?
explanation.explanation: The Russian Revolution took place in 1917, overthrowing the Tsarist autocracy.

explanation.text: Which of the following are considered ancient Indian texts? (Select all correct)
explanation.explanation: The Vedas, Upanishads, and Mahabharata are foundational ancient Indian texts.

explanation.text: Assertion: India is called a Republic. Reason: The head of the Indian state is an elected President rather than a hereditary monarch.
explanation.explanation: Both statements are true. A republic is a form of government where the country is considered a public matter and the head of state is elected.

explanation.text: Match the ancient civilizations with the river valleys where they developed:
explanation.explanation: Early human civilizations primarily developed along major river systems which provided water and fertile soil.

explanation.text: Who is credited with discovering the direct sea route from Europe to India in 1498?
explanation.explanation: Vasco da Gama reached Calicut in 1498, establishing the first direct sea route from Europe to India.

explanation.text: The Constitution of India is the longest written national constitution in the world.
explanation.explanation: With a preamble, hundreds of articles, and 12 schedules, the Indian Constitution is the longest written national constitution.

explanation.text: The First Battle of Panipat was fought in the year ________.
explanation.explanation: The First Battle of Panipat took place in 1526 between Babur and Ibrahim Lodi, marking the beginning of the Mughal Empire in India.

explanation.text: Which of the following are Fundamental Rights guaranteed by the Indian Constitution? (Select all correct)
options: B. Right to Freedom of Religion
explanation.explanation: Right to Property was removed as a Fundamental Right by the 44th Amendment, and Right to Strike is a legal right, not a fundamental one.

explanation.text: Assertion: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre of 1919 caused widespread outrage across India. Reason: General Dyer ordered his troops to fire on an unarmed crowd of men, women, and children.
explanation.explanation: Both statements are correct, and the brutal nature of the massacre directly caused the national outrage.

explanation.text: Match the Indian dynasties with their respective founders:
explanation.explanation: These are the historical founders of three of the most significant empires in Indian history.

explanation.text: Who gave the famous slogan 'Give me blood, and I shall give you freedom'?
explanation.explanation: Subhas Chandra Bose delivered this famous quote to motivate the Indian National Army.

explanation.text: Apartheid was an institutionalized system of racial segregation in South Africa.
explanation.explanation: Apartheid was a policy that governed relations between South Africa's white minority and nonwhite majority from 1948 until the 1990s.

explanation.text: The capital city of the ancient Mauryan Empire was ________.
explanation.explanation: Pataliputra, near modern-day Patna, served as the capital of the Mauryan Empire.

explanation.text: Who among the following were prominent founding leaders of the Non-Aligned Movement? (Select all correct)
explanation.explanation: Nehru (India), Tito (Yugoslavia), and Nasser (Egypt) were key architects of the Non-Aligned Movement.

explanation.text: Assertion: The League of Nations ultimately failed to prevent the outbreak of World War II. Reason: The League lacked an armed force of its own and several major powers, including the US, were not members.
explanation.explanation: Both statements are true. The structural weaknesses of the League of Nations directly contributed to its failure.

explanation.text: Match the historical documents with the years they were adopted:
pairs: French Declaration of the Rights of Man
explanation.explanation: These are key dates for some of the most important political documents in modern history.

explanation.text: Who served as the first Governor-General of independent India?
explanation.explanation: Lord Mountbatten was the last Viceroy and the first Governor-General of independent India.

explanation.text: The Berlin Wall, which divided East and West Berlin, fell in the year 1989.
explanation.explanation: The fall of the Berlin Wall in November 1989 was a pivotal event marking the end of the Cold War.

explanation.text: The cultural movement known as the Renaissance began in the country of ________.
explanation.explanation: The Renaissance originated in Italian city-states like Florence in the 14th century before spreading across Europe.

explanation.text: Which of the following are the three primary branches of the Indian Government? (Select all correct)
explanation.explanation: The Indian Government is divided into the Executive, Legislative, and Judicial branches to ensure a separation of powers.

explanation.text: Assertion: The Right to Information (RTI) Act promotes transparency and accountability in India. Reason: It empowers citizens to seek information from public authorities.
explanation.explanation: Both statements are correct, and empowering citizens to access information is the exact reason it promotes transparency.

explanation.text: Match the significant Indian battles with their corresponding years:
explanation.explanation: These battles were turning points in Indian history, establishing new power dynamics in the subcontinent.

explanation.text: The political ideology of Fascism was founded by which 20th-century leader?
explanation.explanation: Benito Mussolini founded the National Fascist Party in Italy after World War I.

explanation.text: The Indus Valley Civilization was known for its advanced urban planning and drainage systems.
explanation.explanation: Cities like Harappa and Mohenjo-Daro featured sophisticated grid layouts, baked brick houses, and extensive drainage.

explanation.text: The first Indian woman to become President of the UN General Assembly was Vijaya Lakshmi ________.
explanation.explanation: Vijaya Lakshmi Pandit was elected as the President of the eighth session of the UN General Assembly in 1953.

explanation.text: Which of the following were major causes of the French Revolution? (Select all correct)
options: A. Social inequality and the estate system
B. Severe economic crisis and debt
C. The invention of the printing press
E. The discovery of the Americas
explanation.explanation: Social disparities, a bankrupt treasury, and new philosophical ideas heavily contributed to the outbreak of the French Revolution.

explanation.text: Assertion: Emperor Ashoka eventually abandoned military conquest and embraced Buddhism. Reason: He was deeply moved and horrified by the massive loss of life and destruction during the Kalinga War.
explanation.explanation: Both the assertion and the reason are true, and the bloodshed at Kalinga was the direct catalyst for his transformation.

explanation.text: Match the early Presidents of India with their chronological order of service:
explanation.explanation: These leaders served sequentially as the first three Presidents of independent India.

explanation.text: Which amendment to the Indian Constitution is often referred to as the 'Mini Constitution' due to its comprehensive changes?
explanation.explanation: Enacted during the Emergency in 1976, the 42nd Amendment made widespread changes to the Constitution.

explanation.text: The partition of Bengal, ordered by Lord Curzon, took place in 1905.
explanation.explanation: The partition of Bengal was enacted in 1905, dividing the largely Muslim eastern areas from the largely Hindu western areas.

explanation.text: The ancient center of higher learning, ________ University, was located in present-day Bihar.
explanation.explanation: Nalanda was a renowned Mahavihara (Buddhist monastery) and one of the greatest centers of learning in the ancient world.
